learn.title: Wind Resource Assessment
learn.intro: Wind resource assessment identifies optimal locations for wind farms by analyzing wind speed and patterns. This ensures efficient energy production, reduces costs, and maximizes the potential of wind as a renewable energy source. Accurate assessments are critical for scaling wind energy globally.
learn.c1_text: Developers measure average wind speeds at turbine height (typically 80-100m) using meteorological towers. Sites with sustained winds >7 m/s are ideal. For example, India’s Rajasthan and Tamil Nadu have average wind speeds of 6-7 m/s, making them prime for wind farms.
learn.c2_text: Wind patterns are modeled using historical data and simulations to predict energy output. Tools like WAsP (Wind Atlas Analysis and Prediction System) help developers estimate annual energy production, reducing financial risks. In India, this tech is used to optimize 40 GW+ of installed wind capacity.
learn.c3_text: India aims for 30 GW of wind energy by 2025. Tamil Nadu alone contributes 35% of the country’s wind power. The National Institute of Wind Energy (NIWE) conducts detailed assessments to identify untapped sites, such as Gujarat’s Kutch region.
learn.stat2: Global: 70% of wind farms rely on IRENA’s assessment tools
learn.stat3: India: 25% of wind projects in Tamil Nadu and Gujarat

learn.title: Green Bonds & Climate Finance
learn.intro: Green bonds fund renewable energy projects, attracting investors seeking sustainable returns. They channel capital into solar, wind, and storage, accelerating the global energy transition while aligning financial markets with climate goals.
learn.c1_text: Green bonds are debt instruments earmarked for climate-friendly projects. In 2022, $500B+ was issued globally, funding 50% of renewable energy initiatives. They offer investors tax incentives and ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) compliance.
learn.c2_text: Proceeds finance solar farms, wind parks, and grid upgrades. For example, India’s ₹10,000 crore (≈$1.2B) green bond in 2023 targeted 10 GW of solar and wind projects. This reduces reliance on fossil fuels and lowers emissions.
learn.c3_text: India’s green bond market grew 40% in 2023, with ₹20,000 crore raised. The government mandates 15% of public sector banks’ lending to renewables. Projects like the 5 GW solar park in Rajasthan are funded via these bonds.
learn.stat3: India: 15% renewable energy target in public sector loans
learn.stat4: Global: 50% of green bonds fund wind/solar projects

learn.title: Direct Air Carbon Capture
learn.intro: Direct air carbon capture (DAC) uses renewables to extract CO2 from the atmosphere, mitigating climate change. This technology is vital for achieving net-zero emissions, especially in sectors hard to decarbonize.
learn.c1_text: DAC plants use fans to draw air through filters that trap CO2. Renewable energy powers the process, ensuring a net-negative carbon footprint. For example, Climeworks’ plant in Iceland captures 4,000 tons of CO2 annually using geothermal energy.
learn.c2_text: DAC requires massive energy, making renewables essential. A 1 GW DAC plant needs 10 TWh/year, equivalent to 100 wind turbines. In India, the government plans to fund 10 DAC projects by 2030, using solar power to cut costs by 50%.
learn.c3_text: India’s National Carbon Market aims to incentivize DAC. The first plant in Gujarat will capture 10,000 tons/year, reducing emissions from industries. Policies like the Production Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme support DAC-Renewable hybrids.
learn.stat3: India: 50% cost reduction via solar integration

learn.title: Solar Inverters Explained
learn.intro: Solar inverters convert DC electricity from panels into AC for grid use. Choosing the right type (string, micro, hybrid) impacts system efficiency, scalability, and reliability, making them essential for solar energy adoption.
learn.c1_text: String inverters connect multiple panels in series. They’re cost-effective for large systems but suffer efficiency losses if one panel is shaded. For example, a 5 kW system with a string inverter costs ~$1,000, saving 20% vs. micro-inverters.
learn.c2_text: Micro inverters attach to each panel, optimizing individual output. This eliminates shading losses, boosting efficiency by 15-25%. In India, micro-inverters are popular for rooftop systems, where panels face varying directions.
learn.c3_text: Hybrid inverters integrate battery storage, enabling off-grid use. In India, hybrid systems reduce grid dependence by 30%, ideal for rural areas. The PM-KUSUM scheme promotes hybrid inverters for 100,000+ farm solar systems.
learn.stat2: Global: 30% of solar systems use micro-inverters

learn.title: Second Life EV Batteries
learn.intro: Second-life EV batteries repurpose used car batteries for grid storage, extending their lifespan and reducing waste. This innovation lowers costs and enhances grid stability, critical for integrating renewables.
learn.c1_text: EV batteries retain 70-80% capacity after 8 years. Companies like Tesla reuse them for home storage, cutting costs by 30%. In India, 100,000+ EV batteries are repurposed annually for grid backup.
learn.c2_text: Second-life batteries stabilize grids by storing excess solar/wind energy. A 10 MW system in Gujarat stores 10 MWh, powering 1,000 homes during peak demand. This reduces reliance on coal plants by 20% in pilot projects.
learn.c3_text: India’s 2022 Battery Waste Management Rules mandate 50% recycling of EV batteries. Startups like Attero recycle 80% of lithium-ion cells, creating 500+ jobs. Second-life batteries are used in 20% of Delhi’s solar grid projects.
learn.stat2: Global: 500 GWh of second-life capacity by 2030

learn.title: Sodium-Ion Batteries
learn.intro: Sodium-ion batteries offer a cheaper, sustainable alternative to lithium-ion. They use abundant materials like sodium, reducing costs and environmental impact, making them ideal for large-scale energy storage.
learn.c1_text: Sodium-ion batteries cost 30% less than lithium-ion, using 10x more abundant sodium. A 5 kWh unit costs ~$200 vs. $300 for lithium. In India, companies like FREYR are piloting sodium-ion for grid storage.
learn.c2_text: Sodium-ion batteries charge faster and are safer, with no thermal runaway risks. They offer 80% efficiency, ideal for solar farms. India’s first sodium-ion plant in Gujarat aims to supply 20% of the country’s grid storage by 2025.
learn.c3_text: India’s National Energy Storage Mission targets 15 GW of storage by 2030. Sodium-ion batteries are prioritized for rural microgrids, reducing costs by 40%. The government offers $10M in grants for startups like Natron Energy.
learn.stat3: India: 40% cost savings in rural grids
